g.text: WIDE-COLUMN: a hybrid between row-store and column-store

text.tspan: DATA MODEL:
table is a SPARSE map: row_key -> column_family -> column_qualifier -> value
Rows can have MILLIONS of columns; different rows can have different columns.
Columns are GROUPED into 'column families' that are stored together physically.
row_key: user_id 'alice'
column family 'profile':
'name' -> 'Alice', 'email' -> 'alice@...', 'joined' -> '2020-01-01'
column family 'tweets' (with timestamp as part of qualifier):
'tweet:2024-01-15T11:00:00' -> 'second tweet'
... thousands more
column family 'follows':
'bob' -> '', 'carol' -> '', ... thousands more
No two users have the same set of columns. That's fine.
u2022 MASSIVE WRITE THROUGHPUT (LSM-based engines)
Cassandra clusters routinely sustain 100Ku20131M writes/sec/node
u2022 LINEAR HORIZONTAL SCALE u2014 just add nodes
u2022 TUNABLE CONSISTENCY per query (Cassandra: ONE, QUORUM, ALL)
u2022 TIME-SERIES-FRIENDLY u2014 wide rows with timestamp columns are natural
for clickstreams, IoT, telemetry, message timelines
u2022 EFFICIENT RANGE QUERIES within a row (clustering columns)
u2022 SPARSE STORAGE u2014 missing columns cost nothing
u2022 Google Bigtable u2014 the original (2006); foundation for many others
u2022 Apache HBase u2014 open-source Bigtable; on Hadoop
u2022 Apache Cassandra u2014 Bigtable-inspired but with Dynamo's consistent hashing
u2022 ScyllaDB u2014 C++ rewrite of Cassandra; very fast
u2022 DynamoDB u2014 Amazon's; wide-column underneath KV API
u2022 Azure Cosmos DB (wide-column API)
